sections.heading: Complications
sections.content: Bleeding (hematemesis or melena): Acute severe bleeding should undergo prompt endoscopic assessment [1]. Widely available endoscopic treatment options include injection therapy, mechanical therapy (eg, endoscopic clips), ablative therapy (eg, argon plasma coagulation), or a combination of methods [404]. Angiographic embolization techniques may be useful where endoscopy is not helpful [406]. EBRT has been shown to effectively manage acute and chronic GI bleeding [407,408]. Proton pump inhibitors can be prescribed but there are no definitive data supporting their use at this time. Palliative gastrectomy is an option in select patients. Malignant gastric obstruction: Primary goals are to reduce nausea and vomiting and allow resumption of oral diet. Treatment options include: endoscopic placement of enteral stent for gastric outlet obstruction or esophageal stent for EGJ/gastric cardia obstruction [409-413]; surgery (gastrojejunostomy [6,409] or gastrectomy in select patients [202]); EBRT; chemotherapy. Endoscopic placement of SEMS is a safe and effective minimally invasive palliative treatment [410-413]. When obstruction cannot be alleviated, venting gastrostomy may be performed for gastric decompression [415]. Pericardiocentesis if ascites present to reduce risk of infectious complications [418,419]. Pain: May be treated with EBRT or chemotherapy. Tumor-related pain should be assessed and treated in accordance with NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. Severe uncontrolled pain following gastric stent placement should be treated with endoscopic removal of the stent [414]. Nausea and vomiting: Treat in accordance with NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis. May be associated with luminal obstruction, so endoscopic or fluoroscopic evaluation should be performed to determine if obstruction is present. Nutritional deficiency following gastrectomy: Lifelong monitoring and management for nutritional sequelae is required, particularly after total gastrectomy. May include vitamin B12, iron, zinc, calcium, and vitamin D deficiencies. Routine supplementation with daily multivitamin/mineral complex, vitamin B12, calcium, and vitamin D is recommended [391-396,400]. Follow-up with appropriate practitioners should be established for lifelong monitoring [GAST-H, GAST-I]. Dumping syndrome: Occurs following total gastrectomy. Early dumping (within 30 minutes of eating) presents with palpitations, diarrhea, nausea, cramps; late dumping (within 2-3 hours) presents with dizziness, hunger, cold sweats, faintness [397]. Management includes frequent meals, diet high in protein and fiber, low in simple carbohydrates/concentrated sweets, avoid fluid consumption with meals [GAST-I]. Chemotherapy: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, leukopenia, anemia) - common with DCF, FLOT, and other platinum-based regimens [304]; Peripheral neuropathy - associated with oxaliplatin and taxanes; duloxetine may be considered for painful neuropathy [GAST-I]; Nausea and vomiting - antiemetics should be given prophylactically during chemoradiation [GAST-G]; Diarrhea - may require anti-diarrheal agents, bulk-forming agents, and diet manipulation [GAST-I]; Fatigue - encourage physical activity and energy conservation measures [GAST-I]; Feeding tube may be considered in select patients undergoing total gastrectomy, especially if postoperative chemoradiation is a likely recommendation [GAST-C]. Immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events - monitor per NCCN Guidelines for the Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities; In the MATTERHORN trial, grade 3 or 4 immune-related AEs were observed in 7% of patients with durvalumab versus 4% with placebo [55]; Nivolumab plus chemotherapy was associated with 16 treatment-related deaths versus 4 with chemotherapy alone in CheckMate 649 [360]; Any-grade immune-related AEs reported in 31% of patients with tislelizumab versus 12% with placebo in RATIONALE-305 [171]. Targeted therapy: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki-associated ILD/pneumonitis occurred in 12 patients resulting in 1 drug-related death in DESTINY-Gastric01 [366]; ILD/pneumonitis occurred in 8 patients with 2 grade 5 events in DESTINY-Gastric02 [367]; Trastuzumab-related cardiac toxicity requires monitoring; Zolbetuximab associated with increased rates of nausea and vomiting, including grade 3 or higher events [173,183]; Ramucirumab associated with higher rates of hypertension (16% vs 8%) compared to placebo [333]. Surgery: Perioperative morbidity and mortality - D2 dissection historically associated with higher complication rates (25% vs 13% for D1 in Dutch trial; P < .001) [207]; Postoperative nutritional intolerance - feeding jejunostomy may be considered.

cards.name: Bleeding (hematemesis or melena)
cards.detail: Acute severe bleeding should undergo prompt endoscopic assessment [1]. Widely available endoscopic treatment options include injection therapy, mechanical therapy (eg, endoscopic clips), ablative therapy (eg, argon plasma coagulation), or a combination of methods [404]. Angiographic embolization techniques may be useful where endoscopy is not helpful [406]. EBRT has been shown to effectively manage acute and chronic GI bleeding [407,408]. Proton pump inhibitors can be prescribed but there are no definitive data supporting their use at this time. Palliative gastrectomy is an option in select patients.

cards.name: Malignant gastric obstruction
cards.detail: Primary goals are to reduce nausea and vomiting and allow resumption of oral diet. Treatment options include: endoscopic placement of enteral stent for gastric outlet obstruction or esophageal stent for EGJ/gastric cardia obstruction [409-413]; surgery (gastrojejunostomy [6,409] or gastrectomy in select patients [202]); EBRT; chemotherapy. Endoscopic placement of SEMS is a safe and effective minimally invasive palliative treatment [410-413]. When obstruction cannot be alleviated, venting gastrostomy may be performed for gastric decompression [415]. Pericardiocentesis if ascites present to reduce risk of infectious complications [418,419].

cards.name: Pain
cards.detail: May be treated with EBRT or chemotherapy. Tumor-related pain should be assessed and treated in accordance with NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. Severe uncontrolled pain following gastric stent placement should be treated with endoscopic removal of the stent [414].

cards.name: Nausea and vomiting (disease-related)
cards.detail: Treat in accordance with NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis. May be associated with luminal obstruction, so endoscopic or fluoroscopic evaluation should be performed to determine if obstruction is present.

cards.name: Nutritional deficiency following gastrectomy
cards.detail: Lifelong monitoring and management for nutritional sequelae is required, particularly after total gastrectomy. May include vitamin B12, iron, zinc, calcium, and vitamin D deficiencies. Routine supplementation with daily multivitamin/mineral complex, vitamin B12, calcium, and vitamin D is recommended [391-396,400]. Follow-up with appropriate practitioners should be established for lifelong monitoring [GAST-H, GAST-I].

cards.name: Dumping syndrome
cards.detail: Occurs following total gastrectomy. Early dumping (within 30 minutes of eating) presents with palpitations, diarrhea, nausea, cramps; late dumping (within 2-3 hours) presents with dizziness, hunger, cold sweats, faintness [397]. Management includes frequent meals, diet high in protein and fiber, low in simple carbohydrates/concentrated sweets, avoid fluid consumption with meals [GAST-I].

cards.name: Myelosuppression (neutropenia, leukopenia, anemia)
cards.detail: common with DCF, FLOT, and other platinum-based regimens [304]

cards.name: Peripheral neuropathy
cards.detail: associated with oxaliplatin and taxanes; duloxetine may be considered for painful neuropathy [GAST-I]

cards.name: Nausea and vomiting (chemotherapy)
cards.detail: antiemetics should be given prophylactically during chemoradiation [GAST-G]

cards.name: Diarrhea
cards.detail: may require anti-diarrheal agents, bulk-forming agents, and diet manipulation [GAST-I]

cards.name: Fatigue
cards.detail: encourage physical activity and energy conservation measures [GAST-I]

cards.name: Immune-related adverse events (general)
cards.detail: monitor per NCCN Guidelines for the Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities

cards.name: Grade 3 or 4 immune-related AEs (durvalumab)
cards.detail: In the MATTERHORN trial, grade 3 or 4 immune-related AEs were observed in 7% of patients with durvalumab versus 4% with placebo [55]

cards.name: Treatment-related deaths (nivolumab)
cards.detail: Nivolumab plus chemotherapy was associated with 16 treatment-related deaths versus 4 with chemotherapy alone in CheckMate 649 [360]

cards.name: Any-grade immune-related AEs (tislelizumab)
cards.detail: Any-grade immune-related AEs reported in 31% of patients with tislelizumab versus 12% with placebo in RATIONALE-305 [171]

cards.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki-associated ILD/pneumonitis
cards.detail: occurred in 12 patients resulting in 1 drug-related death in DESTINY-Gastric01 [366]; ILD/pneumonitis occurred in 8 patients with 2 grade 5 events in DESTINY-Gastric02 [367]

cards.name: Trastuzumab-related cardiac toxicity
cards.detail: requires monitoring

cards.name: Zolbetuximab-associated nausea and vomiting
cards.detail: associated with increased rates of nausea and vomiting, including grade 3 or higher events [173,183]

cards.name: Hypertension (ramucirumab)
cards.detail: associated with higher rates of hypertension (16% vs 8%) compared to placebo [333]

cards.name: Perioperative morbidity and mortality (D2 dissection)
cards.detail: D2 dissection historically associated with higher complication rates (25% vs 13% for D1 in Dutch trial; P < .001) [207]

cards.name: Postoperative nutritional intolerance
cards.detail: feeding jejunostomy may be considered
